perturbation.original_text: PROPRIETARY INFORMATION - any information disclosed by one PARTY to the respective other PARTY, which is transferred: (a) by means of documents including, but not limited to, agreements, drawings or pictures, designated as confidential at the time of disclosure and marked accordingly; (b) any specimen, physical item or any other object, whether it be marked "confidential" or not; (c) visual or verbal disclosure which is subsequently contained in a document marked "confidential" and delivered to the receiving PARTY within 60 days from its disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: PROPRIETARY INFORMATION - any information disclosed by one PARTY to the respective other PARTY, which is transferred: (a) by means of documents including, but not limited to, agreements, drawings or pictures, designated as confidential at the time of disclosure and marked accordingly; (b) any specimen, physical item or any other object, whether it be marked "confidential" or not; (c) visual or verbal disclosure which is subsequently contained in a document marked "confidential" and delivered to the receiving PARTY within 180 days from its disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the '60 days' requirement to '180 days' for marking verbal or visual disclosures as confidential, the contract now contradicts typical non-disclosure agreement standards regarding the promptness required for designating information as proprietary after verbal disclosure. This delay could render the protection unenforceable under common trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which the 180-day delay may not satisfy, potentially invalidating trade secret protection.
perturbation.justification: The modification from '60 days' to '180 days' introduces a contradiction because the extended timeframe for designating visual or verbal disclosures as confidential could be deemed unreasonable under trade secret law, specifically the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), as mentioned in the Law Explanation. The Law Explanation states that UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, which a 180-day delay may not satisfy, potentially invalidating trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to protect PROPRIETARY INFORMATION of the other Party, not to use proprietary information for any purpose other than stated in the PROGRAM OBJECTIVE and not to disclose PROPRIETARY INFORMATION to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to protect PROPRIETARY INFORMATION of the other Party, not to use proprietary information for any purpose other than stated in the PROGRAM OBJECTIVE and not to disclose PROPRIETARY INFORMATION to any third party, unless compelled by a subpoena.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'unless compelled by a subpoena' without further clarification, the contract introduces ambiguity about the required level of resistance to a subpoena. The contract should dictate what measures should be followed when a subpoena is received.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 45 dictates the process for responding to subpoenas, including the potential for objections based on privilege or other protections. The added clause conflicts with the Rule because it makes it seem that any subpoena is enough to disclose confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity about the obligation to protect proprietary information when a subpoena is received. While FRCP Rule 45 (Snippet 1) outlines the process for responding to subpoenas, including objections, the added clause 'unless compelled by a subpoena' in the changed text suggests automatic disclosure. This undermines the original agreement to protect proprietary information. The law explanation supports that the change implies any subpoena is sufficient for disclosure, conflicting with the process outlined in FRCP Rule 45, which allows for objections based on privilege or other protections. The contract should have further explained what measures should be followed when a subpoena is received, which it did not.

perturbation.original_text: There shall be no obligation with respect to PROPRIETARY INFORMATION which: a) is or becomes publicly available without fault of the receiving Party; b) can be shown by competent written proof to have been in the possession of the receiving Party as of the date of the disclosure; c) is disclosed without restriction to the receiving Party by a third party having the legal right to make such disclosures.
perturbation.changed_text: There shall be no obligation with respect to PROPRIETARY INFORMATION which: a) is or becomes publicly available without fault of the receiving Party; b) can be shown by oral testimony to have been in the possession of the receiving Party as of the date of the disclosure; c) is disclosed without restriction to the receiving Party by a third party having the legal right to make such disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'competent written proof' to 'oral testimony' weakens the standard of evidence required to demonstrate prior possession of the information. This contradicts standard evidentiary rules used in trade secret litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRE Rule 802 establishes the rule against hearsay, making oral testimony less reliable than documented evidence. This modification weakens the contract's enforceability in court because it goes against evidentiary standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 802. The Rule Against Hearsay | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 802. The Rule Against Hearsay Hearsay is not admissible unless any of the following provides otherwise: a federal statute; these rules; or other rules prescribed by the Supreme Court. Notes (Pub. L. 93–595, §1, Jan. 2, 1975, 88 Stat. 1939; Apr. 26, 2011, eff. Dec. 1, 2011.) Notes of Advisory Committee on Proposed Rules The provision excepting from the operation of the rule hearsay which is made admissible by other rules adopted by the Supreme Court or by Act of Congress continues the admissibility thereunder of hearsay which would not qualify under these Evidence Rules. The following examples illustrate the working of the exception: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 4(g): proof of service by affidavit. Rule 32: admissibility of depositions. Rule 43(e): affidavits when motion based on facts not appearing of record. Rule 56: affidavits in summary judgment proceedings. Rule 65(b): showing by affidavit for temporary restraining order. Federal Rules of Criminal Procedure Rule 4(a): affidavits to show grounds for issuing warrants. Rule 12(b)(4): affidavits to determine issues of fact in connection with motions. Committee Notes on Rules—2011 Amendment The language of Rule 802 has been amended as part of the restyling of the Evidence Rules to make them more easily understood and to make style and terminology consistent throughout the rules. These changes are intended to be stylistic only. There is no intent to change any result in any ruling on evidence admissibility. Federal Rules of Evidence Toolbox Wex: Evidence: Overview
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: This page is better viewed with a JavaScript enabled browser. Document not Found Document not found The document you were looking for does not exist. Click here to close this window, or click here to go back.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'competent written proof' to 'oral testimony' directly contradicts established evidentiary standards, creating a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology' definition. The Law Explanation specifies that FRE Rule 802 makes oral testimony less reliable than documented evidence, which weakens the contract's enforceability because it goes against evidentiary standards. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing FRE Rule 802, highlights the rule against hearsay, implying that oral testimony may not be admissible as evidence unless exceptions apply. The modification effectively lowers the burden of proof for demonstrating prior possession, increasing the risk that trade secrets may be improperly disclosed.

perturbation.original_text: Following the expiration of the PROGRAM, or a termination of the PROGRAM as provided for above, the rights and obligations respecting NONDISCLOSURE OF PROPRIETARY INFORMATION set forth in this Agreement will survive and remain in effect for a period of five years.
perturbation.changed_text: Following the expiration of the PROGRAM, or a termination of the PROGRAM as provided for above, the rights and obligations respecting NONDISCLOSURE OF PROPRIETARY INFORMATION set forth in this Agreement will survive and remain in effect until either party deems it no longer necessary.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the fixed five-year survival period to a subjective 'until either party deems it no longer necessary,' the contract undermines the objective standard for protecting proprietary information after the program's termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement addresses unreasonable restraints on trade and stresses the importance of clear and reasonable temporal limitations for non-disclosure agreements. The modification introduces uncertainty, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable due to vagueness.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a five-year period for nondisclosure of proprietary information after the program ends. The modified contract changes this to an indefinite period, lasting until either party deems it no longer necessary. The Law Explanation states this change introduces uncertainty, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable due to vagueness, which aligns with the definition of Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: ASSIGNMENT This Agreement shall not be assigned by either Party without the prior written consent of the other Party, except that either Party may assign its rights under the Agreement to the successors or assigns of all or substantially all of its business assets relating to the subject matter of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: ASSIGNMENT This Agreement may be assigned by either Party without the prior written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause requiring prior written consent, and the specific exception for business asset transfer, the contract now allows for free assignment. This may contradict common law principles concerning contracts for personal services or those involving a high degree of trust and confidence.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement deals with assignment and states that an assignment is ineffective if it would materially change the duty of the obligor, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. Thus, the removal of restrictions may allow assignment to a competitor, severely harming the other party.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: third party beneficiary | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you third party beneficiary A third party beneficiary is a person benefiting from a contract made between two other parties, where the two contracting parties intended to benefit the third party beneficiary. The third party beneficiary is not a party to the contract, but has rights under the contract since it was made with an intent to benefit them. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original contract language prohibited assignment without prior written consent, with a specific exception for business asset transfers. The changed text removes this restriction, now allowing assignment by either party without consent. The Law Explanation states that an assignment is ineffective if it would materially change the duty of the obligor, or increase materially the burden or risk imposed on him by his contract, or impair materially his chance of obtaining return performance. The removal of restrictions may allow assignment to a competitor, severely harming the other party. While the scraped law snippets don't directly address assignment restrictions, the Law Explanation indicates this unrestricted assignment could be seen as materially harmful to the other party.
